_meta.report_name: RBI ATM/POS Payment Infrastructure — System Model (First FOUNDATION)
_meta.status: DRAFT — pending acceptance before writing system_model.json
review_notes: v2.0 spec: static/dynamic split applied — entity descriptions made structural, current-period numbers removed
signal_ids added to all entity nodes; signal_evidence added to all force nodes
polarity field added to all edges; mechanism replaces label
loops array added: 4 identified loops (2 reinforcing, 2 balancing)
entity_atm_network split into entity_atm_onsite + entity_atm_offsite (structurally divergent)
force_rbi_card_lifecycle_norms promoted to inference (RBI Master Direction 12156 Section 8)
force_ncmc_transit_rollout: two competing hypotheses documented (NCMC/transit + RuPay-credit-on-UPI)
risk_cc_market_concentration corrected to status: active (first FOUNDATION, was never previously active)
entity_dc_atm_txn→entity_dc_ecom_txn retyped from contrasts_with to reroutes_to
gap_upi_transaction_data: data_acquisition gap — NPCI publishes monthly UPI data; actionable Layer 1 task
Unconnected subsystem note: infrastructure and debit_card_txn sub-graphs are sparsely connected — this reflects structural reality

nodes.label: Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI)
nodes.description: The Government of India and NPCI established zero merchant discount rate on UPI transactions, reaffirmed through Union Budget 2022-23 provisions. Zero MDR makes UPI QR acceptance structurally cheaper than card POS acceptance for merchants — no terminal hardware cost, no per-transaction fee. Creates a persistent economic incentive that drives QR deployment and suppresses POS terminal investment.
nodes.non_observable_reason: A discrete government policy decision on MDR; not a time-series column in the ATM/POS CSV
nodes.source: Union Budget 2022-23 — MDR waiver provisions for UPI and RuPay; NPCI product overview
nodes.source_excerpt: NPCI confirms zero MDR for UPI transactions for merchants; merchant discount rate not applicable to UPI-based payments
nodes.source_rationale: Zero MDR creates a direct economic incentive for merchant QR preference over POS. 16-period trend shows UPI QR growing 131M while POS contracted from peak — confirming the policy effect

nodes.label: RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026)
nodes.description: RBI directed Paytm Payments Bank Ltd to cease accepting deposits and new credit transactions from February 2024. As the largest single issuer of payment bank debit cards, the wind-down caused an institutional exit from the debit card market, triggering a large single-period debit card purge and payment bank share collapse.
nodes.non_observable_reason: A discrete regulatory enforcement action against a specific institution; not a time-series column in the ATM/POS CSV
nodes.source: RBI Press Release 2024-2025/1589 — Paytm Payments Bank Limited: directions under section 35A
nodes.source_excerpt: RBI directs Paytm Payments Bank to stop accepting new deposits or top-ups with effect from February 29, 2024
nodes.source_rationale: RBI press release directly confirms the enforcement action; debit card base contraction and payment bank share collapse in Apr 2026 data confirms the impact

nodes.label: RBI credit card lifecycle management guidelines (mandatory inactive card closure)
nodes.description: RBI Master Direction on Credit Card and Debit Card Issuance (RBI/2022-23/86) Section 8 requires card issuers to close credit cards inactive for a Board-defined period and to exclude such cards from outstanding counts. This creates periodic step-downs in reported outstanding that do not represent demand contraction — purges of inactive inventory, with recovery as new issuance resumes.
nodes.non_observable_reason: Regulatory guidance on card lifecycle management; not a time-series column in the ATM/POS CSV
nodes.source: RBI Master Direction on Credit Card and Debit Card Issuance (RBI/2022-23/86), Section 8
nodes.source_excerpt: Credit card issuers shall close credit card accounts that have not been used for a continuous period as per Board-approved policy and shall not count such cards in their outstanding card base
nodes.source_rationale: Master Direction Section 8 directly mandates inactive card closure. The periodic purge-and-recovery pattern in cc-outstanding is consistent with this regulatory requirement

nodes.label: Embedded card payment infrastructure rollout (transit NFC or RuPay-credit-on-UPI)
nodes.description: A new embedded payment use case is driving explosive growth in the credit card 'other' transaction category. Two competing hypotheses: (1) NCMC/NFC transit — tap-to-pay at metro fare gates and urban transit creates embedded credit card use at transit touchpoints; (2) RuPay credit on UPI — NPCI's 2022-23 circular permitting credit card transactions via UPI may classify in the 'other' channel, and would be system-wide rather than metro-limited. Hypothesis 2 is more parsimonious geographically. gap_cc_other_composition documents why this cannot be resolved from the current data.
nodes.non_observable_reason: Physical NFC transit infrastructure or UPI-linked credit card payment flows; not directly a time-series column in the ATM/POS CSV
nodes.source_rationale: Two hypotheses documented: (1) NCMC/transit — verify via NPCI NCMC page or MoHUA metro NFC documentation; (2) RuPay-credit-on-UPI — verify via NPCI/RBI 2022-23 circular on UPI-linked credit lines. Whichever is confirmed should be used to promote this node or split into two forces. 24-month hypothesis clock starts at this FOUNDATION.

nodes.label: POS Terminal Network
nodes.description: Physical card-acceptance terminals deployed at merchant locations. The primary infrastructure for credit and debit card transactions. Network size is determined by merchant economics: MDR revenue versus terminal deployment and maintenance costs. Subject to structural substitution pressure from QR-based acceptance where zero-MDR economics apply.

nodes.label: UPI QR Code Infrastructure
nodes.description: QR code-based payment acceptance enabling UPI transactions at merchant points. Zero hardware cost for merchants, zero MDR on transactions. The dominant payment acceptance infrastructure in India by count. Transaction throughput (UPI transaction volumes and values) is not captured in this pipeline — see gap_upi_transaction_data.

nodes.label: Onsite ATMs (branch-based)
nodes.description: Cash dispensing machines installed at bank branch premises. Serve branch banking customers. Operationally subsidised by branch infrastructure — not subject to standalone cash-access deployment economics. Structurally stable compared to offsite ATMs; decline in cash usage has not yet made branch-based ATMs economically unviable.

nodes.label: Offsite ATMs (standalone cash-access points)
nodes.description: Cash dispensing machines at standalone locations (shopping areas, transit points, residential areas). Subject to pure deployment economics: installation cost versus cash dispensing fee revenue. Structurally sensitive to cash usage decline — as consumers shift to digital payments, cash-out frequency falls and standalone ATM economics deteriorate.

nodes.label: Micro ATM Network
nodes.description: Handheld point-of-assistance devices operated by Business Correspondents enabling cash withdrawal and basic banking services. Serve rural and semi-urban populations where full ATM or branch deployment is uneconomical. A complementary cash distribution channel that extends banking reach below the branch-ATM infrastructure threshold.

nodes.label: Credit Card Outstanding Stock
nodes.description: Total credit cards issued and outstanding across all scheduled commercial banks and payment institutions. A stock measure of the potential credit card transaction base. Segmented by bank type (private, PSB, payment bank). Subject to periodic step-downs from mandatory inactive card closures under RBI lifecycle guidelines, followed by recovery as new issuance resumes.

nodes.label: Debit Card Outstanding Stock
nodes.description: Total debit cards issued and outstanding across all banks and payment institutions. The larger stock base, dominated by PSBs. Subject to decline through institutional exits (payment bank closures) and lifecycle purges. A measure of formal banking payment infrastructure reach — particularly relevant for cash-access and low-income segments.

nodes.label: Credit Card POS Transactions
nodes.description: Credit card transactions at physical POS terminals. A flow measure of card-present spending. Volume and value are capacity-constrained by the POS terminal network size. Responds positively to credit card stock growth and merchant acceptance expansion.

nodes.label: Credit Card Ecommerce Transactions
nodes.description: Credit card transactions on ecommerce platforms and online merchants. A card-not-present digital spending channel. The historically dominant credit card transaction channel by value. Not dependent on physical terminal infrastructure, but responds to credit card stock growth and ecommerce platform growth.

nodes.label: Credit Card 'Other' Transactions (unclassified channel)
nodes.description: Credit card transactions in the unclassified 'other' channel. Structurally embedding as a new use case over 12+ months of consecutive growth. Likely driven by either NFC transit tap-to-pay or RuPay credit card on UPI flows — composition cannot be determined from this pipeline (see gap_cc_other_composition and force_ncmc_transit_rollout).

nodes.label: Debit Card ATM Withdrawals
nodes.description: Debit card cash withdrawal transactions at ATM machines. Measures cash demand expressed through the formal banking card channel. Structurally sensitive to the availability of digital payment alternatives — as merchants accept UPI/cards, the need for pre-withdrawal cash decreases. A declining channel as digital payment infrastructure expands.

nodes.label: Debit Card POS Transactions
nodes.description: Debit card transactions at physical POS terminals. A measure of debit card spending at merchants. Stable in share — debit POS serves a different use case than credit POS (lower ticket, more transactional, less credit-dependent). Constrained by POS terminal availability.

nodes.label: Debit Card Ecommerce Transactions
nodes.description: Debit card transactions on ecommerce platforms. Growing as digital-native purchases by debit card holders expand. Absorbs demand formerly channelled through ATM cash withdrawals — consumers increasingly pay online directly rather than withdrawing cash for physical purchases.

nodes.label: UPI transaction volumes and values not in this pipeline
nodes.description: UPI is the dominant Indian payment channel by transaction count but its transaction values and volumes are not in the ATM/POS consolidated CSV. This pipeline measures UPI QR infrastructure (count) but not UPI throughput. Without this data, the card-to-UPI substitution rate cannot be quantified and the infrastructure bifurcation cannot be fully characterised. NPCI publishes monthly UPI statistics — this is an actionable Layer 1 data acquisition task, not a structural limitation.

nodes.label: Credit card 'other' transaction category is not sub-classified
nodes.description: The 'other' credit card transaction category is not sub-classified in the source data. Its composition (NFC transit, RuPay-credit-on-UPI, vending, merchant-initiated) cannot be verified from this pipeline. This prevents resolving the force_ncmc_transit_rollout hypothesis and quantifying which payment use case is driving the structural growth.

nodes.label: Card transactions do not separate international from domestic
nodes.description: POS and ecommerce transaction data combines domestic and cross-border card usage. Cannot separate travel/cross-border spending from domestic consumption. Limits interpretation of ecommerce and POS growth signals.

nodes.label: Payment acceptance bifurcation: POS contracting while UPI grows
nodes.description: POS terminal network contraction while UPI QR expands creates an acceptance gap: merchants who drop POS terminals cannot accept international cards, EMI transactions, premium credit card features, or POS-authenticated payments. Customers without UPI-linked accounts face reduced options at merchants who have switched fully to UPI.
nodes.source: ATM/POS consolidated CSV — pos-terminals declining from peak while upi-qr growing; pos-terminals-pos-streak reset to zero
nodes.source_rationale: Multi-period sustained divergence between POS contraction and UPI QR growth constitutes a structural acceptance bifurcation observable in L1 signals

nodes.label: Debit card base structural decline reducing cash access for underbanked
nodes.description: The debit card base is contracting through payment bank exits and lifecycle purges. This reduces the potential transaction base for populations that depend on debit cards for cash access and digital payments — particularly lower-income and rural segments without credit cards or reliable UPI-linked accounts.
nodes.source: ATM/POS consolidated CSV — dc-outstanding-abs declining; payment bank share collapsing
nodes.source_excerpt: Debit card outstanding declining from peak with payment banks losing 2.3pp share in Apr 2026
nodes.source_rationale: Multi-period decline concentrated in payment banks indicates structural contraction beyond cyclical variation

nodes.label: Credit card market concentrated in top-5 private banks
nodes.description: The top-5 private banks control approximately 71% of credit card outstanding. The largest single issuer holds ~20% of the market. This concentration creates systemic fragility — as demonstrated when a single payment bank's exit caused a market-wide debit card step-down. Similar disruption from a major credit card issuer would have proportionally larger effects.
nodes.source: ATM/POS consolidated CSV — cc-bank-scan, cc-private-share, cc-psb-share signals
nodes.source_excerpt: Private banks: 71.0% credit card share; PSBs: 29.0%; competitive landscape stable across ≥2 periods
nodes.source_rationale: Market share data directly observed across ≥2 periods; concentration risk inference from Paytm debit precedent

nodes.label: Credit card penetration deepening — PSB share gap is the opening
nodes.description: Credit cards outstanding (~119M) represents approximately 11-12% of the debit card base (~1B+). PSBs hold ~64% of debit cards but only ~29% of credit cards — a structural share gap exploitable through debit-to-credit card upgrade offers to existing bank customers. The acquisition cost of upgrading an existing debit card customer to a credit card is materially lower than cold acquisition.
nodes.source: ATM/POS consolidated CSV — cc-outstanding-abs, dc-outstanding-abs, cc-psb-share, dc-psb-share signals
nodes.source_excerpt: Credit cards: ~119M; Debit cards: ~1.02B; PSBs: 29% cc share vs ~64% dc share
nodes.source_rationale: Share gap directly computed from data; PSB upgrade opportunity is a standard bank cross-sell inference

nodes.label: Embedded transit and tap-to-pay payment products
nodes.description: The structural embedding of a new credit card use case (the 'other' category) signals a platform land-grab window for card issuers and acquirers who integrate with the winning infrastructure — whether transit NFC or UPI-linked credit. The opportunity is real regardless of which force hypothesis is confirmed; the appropriate platform strategy differs based on which force is validated.
nodes.source_rationale: Opportunity is tied to force_ncmc_transit_rollout which remains hypothesis. Promote to inference when force hypothesis is resolved. Cycle 1 of hypothesis clock starts at this FOUNDATION.

nodes.label: Rural cash distribution via Micro ATM / Business Correspondent networks
nodes.description: Micro ATM growth counter-trending against overall ATM network contraction signals persistent rural and semi-urban cash demand at a scale below the economics of full ATM deployment. Banks and fintechs expanding Business Correspondent channels with micro ATM deployment can serve this demand at lower capex than branch or full ATM infrastructure.
nodes.source: ATM/POS consolidated CSV — micro-atms-abs growing while atm-offsite-abs declining
nodes.source_excerpt: Micro ATMs growing while offsite ATMs contracting — observed across ≥2 periods
nodes.source_rationale: Counter-trend growth directly observed. BC network opportunity is a standard inference from the infrastructure access pattern

edges.mechanism: Zero MDR makes UPI QR economically superior to POS for merchants; directly drives rapid QR deployment at merchant acceptance points
Zero MDR removes the merchant revenue incentive to maintain POS terminal infrastructure; merchants rationally prefer zero-cost QR alternative
As merchant UPI acceptance expands, consumers need to pre-withdraw cash less frequently before purchases
Economic incentive to drop POS while growing UPI progressively widens the acceptance gap for card-only transactions
Institutional exit of the largest payment bank debit card issuer forced cancellation of its entire debit card portfolio
Paytm exit was the largest single event accelerating structural debit card base erosion
Mandatory closure of inactive credit cards creates periodic step-downs in reported outstanding — purges of inactive inventory unrelated to demand contraction
New embedded payment infrastructure (transit NFC tap-to-pay or RuPay-credit-on-UPI) creates a structural credit card use case in the 'other' transaction category
Structural embedding of a new payment use case creates a platform land-grab window for issuers and acquirers who integrate with the dominant infrastructure
UPI QR acceptance directly substitutes POS terminal need at merchant acceptance points — same merchant function, structurally different economics
Structural observation: offsite ATMs declining as standalone cash-access economics deteriorate, while onsite branch ATMs remain stable — divergent trajectories driven by different cost structures
Structural observation: full ATM network contracting while micro ATM grows — two divergent cash distribution strategies serving different market segments
Structural observation: credit card stock expanding while debit card stock contracts — opposite trends driven by different forces operating simultaneously
Tap-to-pay and transit flows in 'other' category are displacing traditional POS category classification as NFC tap replaces inserted/swiped card transactions at the same terminal
Structural observation: 'other' growing explosively while ecommerce holds share — new payment surface adding volume rather than cannibalising ecommerce
Cash demand formerly expressed through ATM withdrawals is rerouting to digital ecommerce purchases as consumers pay online directly
POS terminal network is the physical capacity constraint on credit card POS transactions — no terminal means no POS transaction at that merchant
POS terminal availability is the structural capacity constraint on debit card POS transaction opportunity
UPI QR acceptance availability reduces consumer need to pre-withdraw cash before purchases — QR merchant reach is correlated with ATM withdrawal decline
UPI QR infrastructure count is measured but UPI transaction throughput is absent from this pipeline — cannot quantify the UPI-vs-card substitution rate
Credit card stock size determines the potential POS transaction base — more active cardholders create proportionally more POS transaction capacity
Credit card stock expansion drives ecommerce transaction capacity — more active cardholders transact more online
Debit card stock contraction reduces the potential ATM cash-withdrawal base — fewer active debit cards means fewer ATM transactions
Debit card base contraction constrains potential ecommerce transaction capacity over time
POS network contraction is the primary observable signal of acceptance bifurcation — each terminal dropped reduces card acceptance at that merchant
Low credit card penetration relative to debit card base, combined with PSB share gap, defines the addressable upgrading opportunity
Sustained debit card base decline reduces formal banking payment infrastructure reach for card-dependent segments
Micro ATM growth signals persistent rural cash demand — opportunity for BC-based cash distribution at lower capex than ATM infrastructure
Other category is measurable but not sub-classified — prevents verification of whether transit NFC, UPI-credit, or other driver is causing the structural growth

loops.label: UPI Network Effect Reinforcing Loop
loops.closure_note: Partially closed within model (zero MDR → QR grows → POS suppressed); closes fully externally through consumer adoption feedback as more merchant QR acceptance drives more consumer UPI usage
loops.description: Zero MDR drives UPI QR deployment and suppresses POS terminals. More QR acceptance points increase consumer UPI adoption. Increased consumer UPI adoption further reduces merchant willingness to maintain POS terminals. Classic network effect reinforcing loop: acceptance drives adoption, adoption drives acceptance.

loops.label: Credit Card Penetration Reinforcing Loop
loops.closure_note: Closes partially within model (card stock → POS txn volume); closes fully through bank revenue enabling more card issuance
loops.description: Credit card stock expansion increases POS transaction volumes. Transaction revenue enables banks to invest in and issue more credit cards. More cardholders create more POS transaction demand. Constrained by POS terminal availability — the reinforcing loop is bounded by the terminal network.

loops.label: Payment Acceptance Bifurcation Balancing Loop
loops.closure_note: Closes externally when acceptance bifurcation reaches a threshold that triggers regulatory intervention or market countermeasures restoring card acceptance economics
loops.description: Zero MDR drives POS terminal contraction. Fewer terminals constrain credit card POS transaction growth, dampening the cc_penetration_R reinforcing loop. As card acceptance degrades and the bifurcation risk intensifies, market or regulatory pressure to restore card acceptance economics may eventually create a countervailing force.

loops.label: Debit Card Base Erosion Balancing Loop
loops.closure_note: Closes externally as debit card base erosion creates financial inclusion policy pressure, which should eventually slow the erosion through regulatory or market response
loops.description: Declining debit card outstanding reduces ATM cash withdrawal capacity, deepening the base erosion risk. As the underbanked population loses payment infrastructure access, financial inclusion pressure builds. This balancing effect should eventually slow the erosion — the risk itself creates the conditions for a corrective response.
